metadata.title: Claude, When Not.
metadata.color_semantics: claude fidelity: INK #3D3929; SPARK #D97757 the one accent. Do not retint.
metadata.greeting_note: HAI persona, shortest-form rule. 'Halo' = Indonesian/German casual, one word. Season finale warmth.
metadata.thesis: Knowing when not to use it IS the AI skill.
metadata.note: ILLUSTRATE LAW reel: UI at B00 (cold open with RESULT lines — the honest not-list), B06 (verdict artifact = the when/not card), B07 (handoff), B08 (outro). Inner beats B01–B05 use distinct custom Remotion scenes on cream stage. PREDICT at B04. Season recap at B05 (HaiSeasonDeck). COLD OPEN, SPARK-LINE, HANDOFF, OUTRO laws in force. Season finale episode — wraps H1–H5 arc. House red line: the one-question test (B03) explicitly teaches the ethical path and names the line plainly without teaching evasion.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: The 'would you hide this from your teacher or boss?' test cut through everything for me, because hiding is the signal that the use is removing something that was supposed to be mine. I want to pressure-test my actual habits against it. Help me: (1) take five specific things I do with Claude this week and run each through the hide-it test, marking scaffold or crutch; (2) explain why skipping the first draft of something I'm meant to learn by drafting counts as skipping the class; (3) turn the H1-through-H5 season into one sentence I can tape to my laptop.

props.command: The 'would you hide this from your teacher or boss?' test cut through everything for me, because hiding is the signal that the use is removing something that was supposed to be mine. I want to pressure-test my actual habits against it. Help me: (1) take five specific things I do with Claude this week and run each through the hide-it test, marking scaffold or crutch; (2) explain why skipping the first draft of something I'm meant to learn by drafting counts as skipping the class; (3) turn the H1-through-H5 season into one sentence I can tape to my laptop.
output: the first draft of anything you're learning to draft
facts you won't verify, decisions you'd hide

beats.narration_text: Use it for leverage. Quizzing yourself on your own material — that's the H3 study stack. Feedback on a draft you wrote — you did the primary work. Explanations for things you're confused about. Practice generation: problems, flashcards, examples. Planning and scheduling — it's an excellent logistician. These are all scaffold uses. You're in the driver's seat.

props.heading: use AI for
items: quizzing yourself on your own material
feedback on a draft YOU wrote
explanations of things you're confused about

beats.narration_text: Don't use it for: graded work without disclosure — that's the line H1 drew. The first draft of anything you're supposed to learn by drafting — the drafting is the learning, and skipping it means you skipped the class. Facts you won't verify — your name on the output means you checked it. Anything you'd hide from your teacher or your boss. That last one is the test.

props.heading: don't use AI for
items: the first draft of anything you're supposed to learn by drafting
anything you'd hide from your teacher or boss

beats.narration_text: The one question that collapses all of it. Would you hide this use from your teacher or your boss? If yes — don't do it. Not because you'll get caught. Because the hiding means the use is removing something that was supposed to be yours. The skill, the learning, the accountability. Hiding is the signal that you know the use is a crutch, not a scaffold. The test isn't about getting caught. It's about who you're becoming.

props.heading: the one question
props.body: Would you hide this use from your teacher or your boss? If yes, don't. Not because you'll get caught — because the hiding means the use is removing something that was supposed to be yours.

beats.narration_text: Commit before the reveal. Which of these is a don't-use? A: ask Claude to quiz you on a chapter. B: ask Claude to write your essay. C: ask Claude to explain a confusing concept. D: ask Claude to give feedback on your draft. One of these removes the learning that's supposed to be yours. Pick it before the next beat.

props.question: Which of these is a 'don't use'? A) Ask Claude to quiz you on a chapter B) Ask Claude to write your essay C) Ask Claude to explain a confusing concept D) Ask Claude to give feedback on your draft
props.commit: commit to B — then the season recap shows you why

beats.narration_text: B. Writing your essay removes the drafting — which was the skill you were supposed to build. A, C, and D are all scaffold uses: quizzing, explaining, giving feedback. The rest of the season laid this out. H1: ask permission, know the line. H2: quiz don't answer — the trade. H3: the quizmaster study stack. H4: conduct, don't compete. H5 is this card. Five pieces, one principle: use it for leverage — not in place of the thing you're supposed to learn.

Let's recap with Claude. The when/not card. Use it for leverage — quizzing, feedback, explanations, practice, planning. Don't use it for drafts you're supposed to write, graded work without disclosure, unverified facts, or anything you'd hide. The one question: would you hide this? The thesis: knowing when not to use it IS the AI skill. That's your card.

artifactLines: USE for: quizzing yourself, feedback on YOUR draft, explanations of confusing concepts, practice generation, planning.
DON'T USE for: graded work without disclosure, first drafts you're supposed to learn by drafting, unverified facts, anything you'd hide.
The one question: would you hide this use from your teacher or boss? If yes — don't. Not fear of being caught. The hiding means the use is taking something that was supposed to be yours.
Knowing when not to use it IS the AI skill.

beats.narration_text: Your move. Paste this prompt in and Claude will print you a wallet-card version of the when/not line. One thing you can keep. Replace the bracket with your actual context.

props.command: Give me a short wallet-card version of when I should and shouldn't use AI as a [student / mid-career professional]. Max 6 bullet points total. Practical and direct.
